- You can open a Precious Metals Account instantly in the app and track your investments.

Answer to your question:
- Yes — you can buy gold through OCBC’s Precious Metals service in the app. Follow these steps as shown in OCBC guidance:

  3. If you don’t have a Precious Metals Account, open one instantly in the app.  
  4. Enter your purchase details — choose between ‘Amount to invest’ or ‘Quantity’.  
  5. Complete the transaction and then track your investment in the app.  
- If you’d like, I can guide you through any of the steps — do you already have a Precious Metals Account with OCBC?

gpt-5-mini.2940026935409283581972797869540221534888:
About Dispute Card Transactions
- Lets you report and dispute incorrect or suspicious card transactions via OCBC Internet Banking or the OCBC app.  
- Protects you from billing errors and helps ensure you are not held liable for unauthorised transactions (subject to card association rules).  
- You must provide any supporting documents requested so OCBC can report the case to the relevant card association (Visa or Mastercard).  
- There are limits on batch disputes: up to 35 Visa transactions or 15 Mastercard transactions at one time; there are no dispute rights for transactions fully authenticated by OTP or OCBC OneToken.

Answer to your question:
- If you see a card transaction that is not yours, report it using the Dispute Card Transactions feature in OCBC Internet Banking or the OCBC app as soon as possible. The policy in the OCBC guidance is: report within 7 days of receiving the statement showing the transaction, or immediately if you received an SMS notifying you of the transaction.  
- OCBC will ask for supporting information or documents so they can raise the dispute with the card association (Visa/Mastercard). Decisions on liability are subject to the card association’s discretion.  
- Note: you cannot dispute transactions that were fully authenticated (for example via OTP or OCBC OneToken), and disputes for lost/stolen cards used in person may be treated differently.  
- If you want, you can also use in‑app card security tools (for example to deactivate your card) while you raise the dispute.

Follow-up question:
- Is the unauthorised transaction shown on your card statement, or did you only receive an SMS alert about it?

What to do next in the app:
- Tap the button below to open Dispute Card Transactions in the OCBC app and start your dispute.

gpt-5-mini.5982962747980698722717514630966141058278:
Solution:
- You can release (unlock) your OCBC Money Lock either at an OCBC ATM/New ATM/Service Kiosk or via Secured Mailbox in the OCBC app/Internet Banking — which option applies depends on whether you are in Singapore or living overseas.

If you are in Singapore (local customers)
- Visit any OCBC ATM, New ATM or Service Kiosk and follow the on‑screen steps:
  1. Insert your ATM/debit/credit card and enter your PIN.  
  2. Navigate to "More Services".  
  3. Tap "Release the funds under Money Lock".  
  4. Select the account to release.  
  5. Tap "Confirm".

If you live overseas (OCBC Singapore customer living overseas)
- Submit a request to release your funds via Secured Mailbox in the OCBC app or Internet Banking:
  1. Log in to the OCBC app > tap "More" > "Secured Mailbox" > tap the "+" icon.  
  2. Choose "Money Lock Release Funds" and indicate you live overseas in your request.  
  - OCBC will be in touch within 4 working days (per the guidance).

Additional important points from the OCBC guidance
- Locked funds must be released in full — partial releases are not allowed. If you need a smaller locked amount afterwards, you must release the full amount then lock a new amount.  
- You cannot release locked funds at an OCBC ATM or branch overseas.  
- Before locking funds, check that your card and PIN remain valid so you can release funds locally if needed. OCBC recommends releasing funds at an ATM before you travel overseas if you may need access while away.  
- Locked funds remain in your account and continue to earn interest; they just cannot be used for withdrawals, GIRO/scheduled/recurring payments, or some other transactions while locked.

What you can do next
- Do you need the ATM steps now, or shall I guide you how to send the Secured Mailbox request? Also, are you currently in Singapore or overseas?

gpt-5-mini.1130525731156713318373573723006218633191:
Solution:
- If you are in Singapore: release must be done at any local OCBC ATM, New ATM, Service Kiosk, or at an OCBC branch. ATM steps:
  1. Insert your ATM, debit or credit card and enter your PIN.  
  2. Go to "More Services".  
  3. Tap "Release the funds under Money Lock".  
  4. Select the account to release and tap "Confirm".
- If you live overseas: you cannot release funds at an OCBC ATM/branch overseas. Submit a request via Secured Mailbox in the OCBC app or Internet Banking (select "Money Lock Release Funds" and state you live overseas). OCBC will be in touch within 4 working days.

Additional information
- Locked funds must be released in full — partial releases are not allowed. If you need a smaller amount released, you must release the full amount first, then submit a new lock request for the smaller amount.
- Check that your card and PIN are valid before locking funds, so you can use them to release funds when needed.
- Locked funds remain in your account balance and continue to earn interest, but they cannot be used for GIRO, scheduled/recurring payments or to pay account fees.

What you can do next
- If you are in Singapore, go to any OCBC ATM, New ATM, Service Kiosk or branch to release your funds.
- If you are overseas, log in to the OCBC app or Internet Banking → More → Secured Mailbox → + → choose "Money Lock Release Funds" and indicate you live overseas.

gpt-5-mini.2319782525846211422507447987034733690520:
About Dispute Card Transactions
- A feature in OCBC Internet Banking and the OCBC app that lets you report and dispute incorrect or unauthorised card transactions.
- Protects you from billing errors and can help you avoid liability for card charges you did not make (subject to rules below).
- You must report unauthorised transactions promptly so OCBC can assist in reporting to the card associations (Visa or Mastercard).
- There are limits and exclusions: you can dispute up to 35 Visa or 15 Mastercard fraud-related transactions at once; transactions fully authenticated (e.g. via OTP or OCBC OneToken) or those made in person with a lost/stolen card generally have no dispute rights. Final decisions rest with the card association.

Answer to your question:
- If a credit card transaction on your card is not yours, you can dispute it through OCBC Internet Banking or the OCBC app. Report it as soon as possible — within 7 days of receiving the statement or immediately after getting an SMS about the transaction — so we can assist you and lodge the dispute with the relevant card association.
- When you submit a dispute, OCBC may ask for supporting information or documents; please provide them so we can proceed on your behalf.
- Important limits and exclusions from the dispute rules:
  - You can dispute up to 35 Visa transactions or 15 Mastercard transactions in one go for fraud-related disputes.
  - There are no dispute rights for transactions that were fully authenticated (for example, via OTP or OCBC OneToken), or for transactions made in person if your card was lost/stolen.
  - All dispute outcomes are subject to the card association’s decision.
- If you want to proceed now, I can help you start the dispute in the app.

Quick question before you proceed:
1) Which card is this — Visa or Mastercard?
2) Did you receive an SMS or see this charge on your latest statement?

What to do next in the app
- Please tap the button below to open Dispute Card Transactions in the OCBC app and start your dispute.

- Report the unauthorised transaction within 7 days of receiving your statement — or immediately if you got an SMS alert — and provide any supporting documents requested.
- You can dispute up to 35 Visa transactions or 15 Mastercard transactions at one time; there are no dispute rights for transactions fully authenticated by OTP or OCBC OneToken. All decisions are subject to the card association’s discretion.

- Important limits and exclusions from the policy:

gpt-5-mini.4376357876836129523968930108307988876244:
Solution:
- How you release locked funds depends on whether you are in Singapore or living overseas.

If you are in Singapore (OCBC customer living in Singapore)
- You must release funds at a local OCBC ATM, New ATM or Service Kiosk, or at an OCBC branch.
- ATM steps:
  1. Insert your ATM, debit or credit card and enter your PIN.  
  2. Navigate to "More Services".  
  3. Tap "Release the funds under Money Lock".  
  4. Select the account to release.  
  5. Tap "Confirm".

If you are an OCBC Singapore customer living overseas
- Submit a request to release your funds via Secured Mailbox in the OCBC app or Internet Banking:
  1. Log in to the OCBC app > More > Secured Mailbox > tap the "+" icon.  
  2. Select "Money Lock Release Funds" from the dropdown and indicate you live overseas.
- OCBC will be in touch within 4 working days.

Additional information
- Locked funds must be released in full — partial releases are not allowed. You would need to release the full amount first, then submit a new lock request if needed.
- Locked funds remain in your account and continue to count toward your balance and earn interest, but cannot be used for GIRO, scheduled/recurring payments or account fees while locked.
- You cannot release your funds at an OCBC ATM or branch overseas. If you plan to travel and may need access, release funds at an OCBC ATM before leaving.

What you can do next
- If you are in Singapore, visit any OCBC ATM/New ATM/Service Kiosk or branch and follow the ATM steps above.
- If you are overseas, submit the Secured Mailbox request in the app or Internet Banking and wait for OCBC to contact you (within 4 working days).
